issue desc: The security configuration permits every request through anyRequest().permitAll(), so all controllers execute without Spring Security enforcing authentication.
The /api/admin/directory/user-search endpoint is reachable without any role or authentication check, allowing non-admin callers to query directory data through DirectoryService.

CWE name: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
issue desc: The /api/users endpoint returns usernames, email addresses, roles, and account status values without any access-control enforcement.
The document read/write endpoints use only the path-supplied publicRef and perform no caller authentication, enabling unauthorized access or overwrite if that reference is obtained.
The application configuration hard-codes the notification API key and LDAP bind credentials.
The notification gateway base URL uses HTTP, so the configured API key and SMS-delivered OTP payloads would traverse the network without transport encryption.
The H2 console is enabled in the shipped configuration, exposing a development-oriented administrative interface in the web context if deployed unchanged.
The startup CommandLineRunner seeds predictable default accounts with the fixed password Password123!, introducing built-in credentials.
Password reset bearer tokens are stored directly in plaintext in the database instead of as one-way verifiers.
The MFA one-time passcode is stored in plaintext in persistent storage, increasing disclosure impact if the database is exposed.
Session bearer tokens are stored in plaintext and later matched directly by SessionAuthService, so database disclosure immediately exposes valid sessions until expiry.
The MFA step-one bearer token used across AuthServiceImpl and MfaServiceImpl is persisted in plaintext, so database disclosure enables takeover of pending authentication flows.
